game_name: Back to the Future Part II & III

design_philosophy.core_loop: Collect scattered items across multiple time periods and return them to their correct temporal locations
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Time pressure combined with item collection across large, unmapped spaces without guidance
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Travel through time as Marty McFly, solving temporal paradoxes by recovering misplaced artifacts
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately punishing design: 30 items to find across three time periods (1955, 1985, 2015) with no maps, passwords, or player aids. The game commits entirely to exploration-based frustration as its core friction point rather than skill-based challenge.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: No difficulty settings or assistance options documented. Players face mandatory item collection with no map or guidance system.

progression.unlock_mechanism: Item collection and placement required to progress through Part II narrative. Part III uses smaller map with fewer items, suggesting reduced complexity for later chapters.
replayability_hooks: Optional puzzle rooms (compass location noted as optional)
Hidden item locations without guidance systems

world_building_techniques: Item placement as narrative breadcrumbs connecting movie plot elements
Licensed IP world recognition (Back to the Future franchise)

faction_indicators: Biff as temporal antagonist (items scattered intentionally)
danger_signals: Item misplacement as world disorder indicator
environmental_storytelling: Items from different eras scattered across time periods signal narrative disruption
Part II's three-era design vs Part III's single-era containment suggests escalating complexity then resolution
Optional puzzle rooms provide secondary world-building content

sources_used: tcrf - The Cutting Room Floor (Back to the Future Part II & III article)
